content.text: JSX expressions evaluate to plain JavaScript objects — React elements. Because they are values, you can assign them to variables, store them in arrays, return them from functions, and pass them as props. There is nothing special about
in this regard; any prop can hold a React element.

attrs.alt: Three patterns: icon slot, header/footer named slots, and component-type prop

content.text: The three most common patterns are shown above: a single-element slot (icon), multiple named slots (header, children, footer), and passing a component type as a prop (
prop). Each is covered in detail below.
The simplest pattern: a prop that holds one React element used as an icon, avatar, or decoration alongside other content.
The component wraps the element in a
to hide it from screen readers (the button label covers it). The element itself can be any size, any icon library, or even a custom SVG —
When a component needs more than one content region, add named props alongside
. This is the JSX equivalent of named slots in other templating systems.
Using named slots instead of putting everything in
has two advantages: the component can apply different styling or behaviour to each region (e.g. the footer gets
automatically), and callers cannot accidentally put footer content inside the body or vice-versa.
Instead of passing an element, you can pass a
— a reference to a function or class — and the receiving component renders it. This is the
is essential: JSX requires the rendered identifier to start with an uppercase letter to distinguish component references from native HTML tag names. If you tried to write
, React would treat it as an unknown HTML element.
JSX elements stored in variables behave exactly the same as elements written inline. This enables conditional slot composition outside JSX:
Pulling complex conditionals out of the return statement keeps JSX readable. The variable is just a React element object until React renders the tree.
A component that accepts an element prop renders it by placing it in JSX as
. React knows it is already a React element and renders it as-is, with the exact props its creator gave it — the receiving component cannot see or modify those props.
This is the key difference from passing a
(Pattern 3): when you pass an element, the props are baked in at the call site. When you pass a type, the receiving component can instantiate it with its own chosen props.
Element vs Type: When to Use Each
Yes — props set at call site
Polymorphic element (as), list item renderer, skeleton loader
Lazy Evaluation: Render Props vs Element Props
— the JSX is evaluated at the call site. A render prop (a function) is
— the component calls the function only when it needs the output. This distinction matters when the element is expensive to create or when the component only renders it conditionally.
For most element props, eager evaluation is fine — React does not actually
the element until it appears in the render output. But if the element's prop computation is expensive (e.g. sorting a large array to produce list items), a render prop defers that cost.
TypeScript Typing for Element and Type Props
TypeScript has specific types for these patterns. Using the right type prevents callers from passing a raw string where an element is expected.
type is complex but provides full type safety: when you write
Passing a type where an element is expected.
passes an instantiated element. The component receives the function object and rendering
produces nothing (React skips functions in JSX).
A component cannot safely add props to a received element without
, which is brittle with Fragments. If you need to inject props, accept a type prop (Pattern 3) or a render prop function instead.
is treated as an unknown HTML element. Always alias to uppercase:
If you build an array of elements and pass them as a prop, each needs a
just like any other mapped JSX.
Building a Page Layout with Named Slots
The following worked example shows a complete page shell that accepts named element props for each region, plus
for the main content area. This pattern is common in application frameworks and admin dashboards.
imports nothing from the feature layer — no
. It only knows about layout. The feature components live entirely inside the page route that uses the shell.
Prop values can be arrays of React elements, not just single ones. This is especially useful for toolbar buttons, breadcrumb items, or menu entries where the count is not known at design time.
When the array comes from a loop and the elements carry meaningful identity, prefer stable keys. In the
above, the key on the wrapper
uses index (acceptable here since the array is static), but if the array is dynamic (buttons can be reordered), use a stable identifier from the data.
Compound Component Pattern via Element Props

Element props are one way to implement compound components without requiring sub-components to be nested inside the parent's JSX. The caller assembles sub-component elements and passes them as explicit props:
inspection. The explicit array approach here is simpler and avoids the fragility of
Tests for components that accept element props follow the same approach as other component tests — you render the component with whatever elements you want to verify:
JSX expressions evaluate to React element objects; any prop can hold one.
Use single element props for icons, avatars, and decorations alongside
Use named slots (header, footer) when a component has multiple content regions.
prop (renamed to an uppercase variable) enables polymorphic components that render any element type.
Pass an element when the caller owns the props; pass a type when the component needs to own them.
